summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on a two-month period where comments were disabled on Coding Horror due to technical issues, and what he learned from the experience. While initially relieved by the silence, he came to miss the community dialogue and realized that comments, despite their scaling problems, are essential to blogging. He acknowledges that at large scale, comments require active moderation or they devolve into chaos. The post announces the return of comments with new restrictions: no anonymous posting and stricter moderation to maintain quality discourse.
key_points: Comments don't scale - at high readership levels, unmoderated comments devolve into chaos and drive away thoughtful participants
The best insights on a blog often come from the comments section, as the community collectively knows more than any individual blogger
Disabling comments created an unexpected benefit: readers were forced to think for themselves rather than being influenced by others' reactions
The psychic burden of reading and moderating hundreds of comments weighs heavily on both writers and readers
Requiring authentication and stricter moderation are necessary trade-offs to maintain quality discourse at scale
money_quotes: Comments don't scale worth a damn.
At a large enough scale, comments require active moderation or they rapidly go sour.
The best part of a blog post often begins where the blog post ends. If you are offended by that, I humbly submit you don't understand why blogs work.
It's an open secret amongst bloggers that the blog comments are often better than the original blog post, and it's because the community collectively knows far more than you or I will ever know.
You mean I can say whatever I want, and nobody can contradict me underneath my very own post? Revolutionary!
In the reverberating echo chamber that is the internet, I think we would all do well to remind ourselves of that periodically.
If what you have to say isn't worth authenticating to post, it's probably best for both of us if you keep it to yourself anyway.
key_insight: Online communities require active curation to thrive - the same openness that enables great discussions also invites dysfunction, making moderation an essential but exhausting responsibility.
